exemplar.title: How can the implementation of ethical objectives address interests of Chipotleâs stakeholders and mitigate further legal, financial, and reputational risks?

detailedExemplarData.title: How can the implementation of ethical objectives address interests of Chipotleâs stakeholders and mitigate further legal, financial, and reputational risks?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

files.fileName: Olivia Carlson - Business Management IA - Final Draft - 11225473 (1).pdf

strengths: The ethical concept of transparency is woven into every major section, from stakeholder analysis through CSR objectives to legal risk, showing sustained integration.
Analysis returns repeatedly to the moral implications of portionâsize deception and shareholder lawsuits, demonstrating nuanced understanding.
Clear linkage between ethical theory and practical recommendations (e.g., utilitarian vs. deontological perspectives) underpins the investigation throughout.
weaknesses: Occasional descriptive background (shrinkflation narrative) momentarily drifts from explicit ethical analysis.
A concise, formal definition of ethics at the outset would strengthen conceptual framing even further.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although minor narrative digressions occur, the paper consistently centers on ethicsâframing research questions, stakeholder mapping, risk assessment and CSR proposals in moral terms. These repeated returns to ethical analysis, coupled with recommendations grounded in ethical theory, meet the descriptor for full integration.

strengths: Bibliographic citations draw on a range of reputable media sources, demonstrating research breadth.
weaknesses: No discrete supporting documents (3â5 primary or secondary sources) are appended.
Lack of appendix with raw data or stakeholder communications prevents assessment of document relevance or depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The rubric requires 3â5 discrete supporting documents appended to the IA. As none are included, this criterion must receive zero despite the quality of cited online articles.

strengths: Stakeholder mapping (Johnson & Scholes grid) and CSR frameworks are selected with clear purpose and depth.
Inclusion of legal risk and reputation theory broadens the analytical toolkit effectively.
Each tool is applied directly to Chipotleâs situation, showing clear relevance to the research question.
weaknesses: Application at times remains descriptive rather than quantitatively evaluated.
Additional theories (e.g., ethical frameworks) could enrich the toolkit further.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Beyond an adequate selection, the inclusion of multiple theories (stakeholder, CSR, legal-ethical, reputation) and clear mapping to Chipotleâs challenges demonstrate effective selection and application, justifying a top-band score.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Arguments are supported by both qualitative (media reports, stakeholder quotes) and quantitative data (share-drop percentages, view counts).
Analysis of ethical objectives includes risk mitigation, feasibility and stakeholder prioritization, showing sustained integration.
Linkages between data, theory and recommendations are consistently made, creating a coherent evaluative thread.
weaknesses: Some assertions (e.g., elimination of certain stakeholders) would benefit from additional concrete evidence.
Greater triangulation with financial or survey data would further reinforce conclusions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation moves beyond âsome analysisâ to a thorough, integrated evaluation of evidence, tools and theory. The layered use of data to support ethical recommendations aligns with the top-band descriptor of sustained, comprehensive analysis.

strengths: Conclusions directly answer the research question, outlining how ethical objectives address stakeholder interests and mitigate risk.
Logical consistency with the body of evidence is maintained, with each recommendation traced back to earlier analysis.
The final synthesis offers actionable steps grounded in the preceding evaluation.
weaknesses: Reliance on mostly qualitative evidence limits depth; inclusion of more quantitative metrics would strengthen robustness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusions are fully consistent with the investigationâs findings and explicitly respond to the research question, satisfying both rubric strands for the highest band.

strengths: Report follows an appropriate academic structure with clear headings, TOC, introduction, analysis sections and conclusion.
Transitions and signposting guide the reader effectively through each thematic section.
weaknesses: Some case-study transitions are abrupt and could use smoother signposting back to Chipotleâs ethical focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: An appropriate, logical structure is maintained throughout, fulfilling the rubricâs requirements for full marks in this criterion.

strengths: All required IA elements (title page, research question, word count, TOC, bibliography, citations, personal code) are present.
Formatting is consistent and professional, supporting readability and adherence to IB guidelines.
weaknesses: A few bibliography entries have minor formatting inconsistencies across lines.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Every required presentation element is included and correctly formatted, meriting the maximum score for presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical structure with introduction, themed sections, conclusion, TOC and references.  Transitions are clear and headings appropriate, satisfying the descriptor for an âappropriate structure throughout.â
Because no supporting documents are supplied, relevance and depth cannot be judged.  The strand therefore earns zero according to the rubric.
Title page, research question, word count, table of contents, bibliography and citations are all present.  Required elements therefore appear complete, earning the highest band within this criterion.

No discrete supporting documents (3â5 pages of extracted sources) are included as required by the IA; the paper only cites online articles in the bibliography.  Therefore the criterion for number of documents is not met.

Stakeholder mapping is applied to classify Chipotleâs groups (Group D, etc.) and CSR is linked to transparency objectives, demonstrating adequate application to the research question.  However, analysis remains largely descriptive and lacks detailed, data-driven evaluation of each toolâs outcomes, so effectiveness is moderate rather than clear and convincing.

Evidence used is mostly qualitative media reports (e.g., 16.5 million views, 7.9 % share drop).  Quantitative data are minimal and not triangulated.  Selection is therefore **superficial** but present, fitting the band-2 descriptor.

The student does analyse how ethical objectives could mitigate risks and links this back to stakeholder theory; however, evaluation of feasibility, cost or prioritisation is quite limited.  The work therefore exhibits âsome analysisâ of the research question, matching band 3.
The conclusion explicitly answers the research question, stating how ethical objectives can address stakeholder interests and mitigate risks.  This meets the top band for this strand.

The student selects stakeholder analysis/mapping and CSR as primary tools, with occasional reference to legal risk and reputation theory.  This constitutes an **adequate** but not wide-ranging toolkit; hence the middle band for selection is justified.

Conclusions are logically consistent with the discussion of ethics and CSR but rely on the same limited, mostly qualitative evidence.  Coherence is acceptable yet not rigorously substantiated, warranting the âsome consistencyâ band.

Chipotle is the clear organisational focus; stakeholder mapping, CSR discussion and legal context are all applied directly to the company.  Analysis links decisions, risks and stakeholder reactions to Chipotleâs operations.  However, occasional diversions to AriZona, General Mills, etc. dilute the depth of integration, preventing the work from reaching the âcomprehensive â fully integratedâ descriptor.  Hence a strong but not top-level band is appropriate.

The student consistently frames the investigation around the key concept of **ethics** and returns to the ethical implications of portion-size deception, shareholder lawsuits, and CSR in each major section.  Discussion goes beyond definition by analysing why lack of transparency is unethical and how ethical objectives could redress stakeholder concerns.  Nevertheless, parts of the paper (e.g., the extended shrinkflation narrative) drift into general background without explicit ethical analysis, so full integration is not sustained throughout.  This aligns with the descriptor of âstrong understanding with partially integrated analysis.â

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and logically ordered, facilitating easy navigation through the reportâs sections.
No discrete supporting documents are appended; to meet the IA requirement, include 3â5 primary or secondary documents in an appendix.
The historical context of Chipotleâs founding and past controversies provides a solid foundation for understanding stakeholder perceptions.
After introducing the shareholder lawsuit, apply stakeholder theory or legalâethical frameworks to discuss how misrepresentation affected each stakeholder's rights.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The paragraph describing the lawsuit ends abruptly; ensure full sentences and elaborate on how the lawsuitâs claims align with ethical breaches.
Including the personal code is a good demonstration of adherence to IB administrative requirements.
The date is provided correctly, meeting the IA formatting expectations.
The research question is clearly articulated and positioned prominently, providing effective guidance for the investigationâs focus.
The concept of ethics is identified but not defined; consider offering a concise definition of âethicsâ and linking it explicitly to each section to strengthen conceptual integration.
The assertion that Chipotle âvirtually eliminatedâ certain stakeholders is bold; provide specific examples or evidence of this exclusion to substantiate the claim.
The Johnson & Scholes powerâinterest grid is presented clearly and enhances understanding of stakeholder prioritization.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Expand discussion by showing how each stakeholder group would respond to proposed ethical objectives rather than generalizing on Group D alone.
The CSR definition is wellâcited and establishes a solid theoretical foundation for ethical objectives.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Consider integrating an ethical framework (e.g., utilitarianism vs deontology) to evaluate proposed ethical objectives and enrich the analysis.
Restating the research question at the close of the introduction effectively reminds the reader of the investigationâs focus.
Quantitative data such as â16.5 million viewsâ and â7.9% share dropâ strengthen the analysis; consider triangulating with financial or survey data.
This descriptive narrative of the portionâsizing scandal lacks explicit ethical analysis; integrate the concept of ethics by evaluating the moral implications of these actions.
The industryâwide discussion of shrinkflation adds context but drifts away from Chipotle; tighten analysis by connecting each example back to the companyâs ethical practices.
External research on shrinkflation aversion from ESADE adds valuable breadth and shows awareness of academic literature.
Transitions into other companiesâ responses are abrupt; improve flow by signposting how each case relates back to ethical transparency in Chipotle.
This sentence draws attention to stakeholder perspectives effectively; consider expanding with direct quotes or survey data where possible.
The conclusion is clearly signposted, allowing readers to easily recognize the reportâs final synthesis.
The introduction to stakeholder analysis is wellâplaced and provides a clear rationale for selecting this tool.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Citing diverse media outlets (CNN, Forbes, Reuters) strengthens the investigationâs credibility and shows engagement with multiple perspectives.
Additional sources are listed but lack commentary on relevance; annotate each with a brief note explaining its contribution to the research.
To satisfy the IA supporting documents strand, append a set of scanned or PDFâformat working documents illustrating raw data or stakeholder communications.
Some bibliography entries are split across lines; ensure each reference follows consistent formatting (author, date, title, source, URL).
